mixed.question: What unusual biological trait do wombats possess regarding their digestive output?
options: Their droppings glow under UV light
Their droppings dissolve in water instantly
mixed.fact: Wombats produce cube-shaped poop due to varying elasticity in their intestinal walls — it helps prevent rolling off rocks!

mixed.question: Which animal can survive being completely frozen solid and then thaw back to life?
mixed.fact: The Arctic woolly bear caterpillar can freeze solid for up to 14 winters before finally pupating into a moth!

mixed.question: True or False: Honey found in 3,000-year-old Egyptian tombs was still edible when discovered.
options: Only partially — it had fermented into mead
mixed.fact: Honey's low moisture, acidity, and natural hydrogen peroxide make it essentially eternal if sealed properly.

mixed.question: In 2025, researchers discovered that which common household pet can recognize an average of 120+ human words?
mixed.fact: A 2025 Eötvös Loránd University study found typical family dogs respond meaningfully to far more words than previously thought.

mixed.question: In which country is it technically illegal to die in the town of Longyearbyen because bodies cannot decompose in the permafrost?
mixed.fact: Longyearbyen in Svalbard stopped burying people in 1950 after discovering bodies weren't decomposing in the frozen ground.

mixed.question: In Samoa, what domestic activity is illegal to forget on a Sunday?
options: Forgetting to turn off your car engine
Forgetting to wear a shirt outdoors
mixed.fact: Samoa has strict Sunday observance laws, and neglecting to feed your animals on the Sabbath can result in a fine.

mixed.question: In Milan, Italy, what facial expression are citizens technically required to maintain in public (except at funerals or hospitals)?
mixed.fact: Milan's city regulations from the Austro-Hungarian era still technically require citizens to smile in public or face a fine!

mixed.question: How many Earths could fit inside the volume of the Sun?
mixed.fact: The Sun is so massive that roughly 1.3 million Earths could fit inside it — yet it's just a medium-sized star.

mixed.question: What surprising phenomenon did NASA's Juno mission confirm happens deep inside Jupiter's atmosphere?
mixed.fact: Helium becomes immiscible with hydrogen at extreme pressures inside Jupiter, forming actual droplets that rain into the interior.

mixed.question: Which recently discovered exoplanet, confirmed in late 2024, orbits three stars simultaneously in a rare triple-star system?
mixed.fact: TIC 290061484 b's triple-star system has the shortest outer orbital period ever found, challenging planet formation theories.

science.question: Which common over-the-counter drug was originally derived from willow bark and used for centuries before Bayer patented it in 1899?
science.fact: Hippocrates wrote about willow bark tea for fevers around 400 BC — millennia before aspirin was synthesized.

science.question: In 2025, researchers identified a new mechanism by which which organ can 'remember' prior infections independent of immune cells?
science.fact: Gut epithelial cells can retain pathogen memory through epigenetic changes, acting as a non-immune defense layer.

science.question: True or False: The bacterium Mycobacterium leprae, which causes leprosy, cannot be grown in standard laboratory culture media to this day.
science.fact: M. leprae is one of the few known pathogens still impossible to grow in artificial lab media.

science.question: Which deadly disease was historically called 'the King's Evil' because royal touch was believed to cure it?
science.fact: English and French monarchs 'touched' thousands of scrofula sufferers in elaborate healing ceremonies for centuries.

science.question: What is the name of the quantum phenomenon where particles that have interacted remain correlated regardless of distance?
science.fact: Einstein famously dismissed entanglement as 'spooky action at a distance,' but experiments have confirmed it's real.

science.question: Which everyday force is technically a manifestation of electromagnetic repulsion between electron clouds in atoms?
science.fact: When you sit in a chair, you never actually touch it — electron clouds repel each other at atomic scales.

science.question: How many times stronger is the strong nuclear force compared to gravity at subatomic scales?
science.fact: Gravity is extraordinarily weak at quantum scales — it takes an entire planet's mass to make you feel it.

science.question: Which deep-sea marine creature has three hearts and blue copper-based blood?
science.fact: Two hearts pump blood past the gills, while the third pumps it to the body — and it's literally blue.

science.question: What is the term for the phenomenon where coral colonies simultaneously release eggs and sperm into the water, often after a full moon?
science.fact: Mass spawning is so synchronized that entire reef systems reproduce within the same hour on one night per year.

science.question: Which marine animal can regenerate its entire body from as little as three-hundredths of its original tissue?
science.fact: A sponge pushed through a fine mesh will reaggregate and rebuild itself into a functional organism within weeks.

history.question: Which ancient Mesopotamian city is believed to have been the world's first city to reach a population of 80,000 people?
history.fact: Around 2900 BCE, Uruk's massive 6 km wall enclosed the largest urban population the world had ever seen.

history.question: What surprising material did ancient Mesopotamians use as an early form of envelope to protect clay tablet messages?
history.fact: Scribes wrapped tablets inside a clay 'envelope' bearing the same text, so the inner tablet stayed tamper-proof.

history.question: True or False: The ancient Sumerians brewed beer using straws because their beer contained floating grain husks and sediment.
options: True, but only royalty used straws
True, but straws were ceremonial only
history.fact: Sumerian art shows communal beer-drinking through long reed straws — some straws were even made of gold.

history.question: In 2025, archaeologists at a Mesopotamian site revealed that the Assyrians likely invented which surprisingly modern technology?
history.fact: The Nimrud lens, re-analyzed in 2025, may be the oldest known purpose-ground optical lens, dating to 750 BCE.

history.question: Which secret CIA project spent $20 million attempting to use psychic powers for military espionage during the Cold War?
history.fact: Project Stargate ran for over 20 years before the CIA concluded 'remote viewing' was too unreliable for intelligence.

history.question: How many nuclear weapons were accidentally lost by the U.S. military during the Cold War and never recovered?
history.fact: These lost nukes are called 'Broken Arrows' — one lies in a swamp in North Carolina to this day.

history.question: Which Soviet leader proposed that the USSR join NATO in 1954, a request that was rejected by Western powers?
history.fact: Molotov's 1954 NATO application was likely a strategic ploy, but its rejection helped justify the Warsaw Pact's creation.

history.question: Which ancient Chinese dynasty first standardized weights, measures, currency, and even axle widths across the realm?
history.fact: Qin Shi Huang even mandated a standard cart axle width so wheels fit the same road ruts everywhere.

history.question: Who was the only female emperor (huangdi) in Chinese history, founding her own short-lived dynasty in 690 CE?
history.fact: Wu Zetian rose from concubine to emperor, establishing the Zhou dynasty and ruling until age 80.

history.question: What did ancient Chinese alchemists accidentally invent while searching for an elixir of immortality during the Tang Dynasty?
history.fact: A 9th-century text warns alchemists not to mix sulfur, charcoal, and saltpeter — essentially the first gunpowder recipe.

popculture.question: Which Netflix series became the platform's most-watched non-English show of all time within its first 28 days of release?
popculture.fact: Squid Game accumulated 1.65 billion viewing hours in its first 28 days, shattering all Netflix records.

popculture.question: True or False: Apple TV+'s 'Severance' Season 2, released in early 2025, became the platform's most-watched season premiere ever.
options: It was second to 'The Morning Show'
popculture.fact: Severance Season 2 broke Apple TV+ records, with its premiere drawing more viewers than any prior original.

popculture.question: What was unusual about the way HBO's 'The Penguin' was classified for awards purposes despite airing on Max?
options: It competed as a film at the Oscars
It was submitted as a limited series despite being a spinoff
It was classified as a documentary
It was ineligible for any awards
popculture.fact: Despite being a Batman spinoff, The Penguin competed as a limited series rather than a drama.

popculture.question: Which streaming show holds the record for the most Emmy nominations received in a single year?
popculture.fact: FX's Shōgun earned 25 Emmy nominations in 2024, the most for any show in a single year.

popculture.question: The 'Hawk Tuah Girl' viral moment of 2024 originated from a street interview in which U.S. city?
popculture.fact: Hailey Welch's Nashville street interview clip amassed over 100 million views within days.

popculture.question: Which 2024 meme involved people editing a famous celebrity's image to appear unexpectedly in historical photos and artworks?
popculture.fact: A Timothée Chalamet lookalike contest in NYC drew hundreds and the real Chalamet actually showed up.

popculture.question: Which viral TikTok sound from a 2024 interview features someone saying 'very demure, very mindful'?
popculture.fact: Jools Lebron's 'demure' videos earned her brand deals, but she couldn't initially trademark the phrase.

popculture.question: At the 2024 Oscars, which film won Best Picture in what many considered a major upset?
popculture.fact: Oppenheimer swept with 7 Oscars, giving Christopher Nolan his first-ever Academy Award wins.

popculture.question: Who became the oldest person ever to win the Academy Award for Best Actress when she won in 2025?
popculture.fact: Demi Moore won her first Oscar at 62 for 'The Substance,' calling it a career resurrection.

popculture.question: Which country's film industry produced the most Best International Feature Film Oscar nominees in the ceremony's entire history?
popculture.fact: Italy has received over 30 nominations for Best International Feature Film, winning the award 14 times.

geography.question: Which African country contains the Danakil Depression, one of the hottest inhabited places on Earth with average temperatures exceeding 34°C year-round?
geography.fact: The Danakil Depression sits 125 meters below sea level with lava lakes and toxic gas fields.

geography.question: True or False: Lake Chad, once one of Africa's largest lakes, has shrunk by approximately 90% since the 1960s.
geography.fact: Lake Chad shrank from 25,000 km² to roughly 1,350 km², displacing millions of people.

geography.question: In 2025, which African nation officially opened the Grand Ethiopian Renaissance Dam's final turbines, completing the controversial megaproject?
geography.fact: The GERD is Africa's largest hydroelectric dam, generating 5,150 MW from the Blue Nile.

geography.question: The Richat Structure, a mysterious 40-km-wide geological formation sometimes called the 'Eye of Africa,' is located in which country?
geography.fact: Once thought to be a meteor impact, it's now believed to be an eroded geological dome.

geography.question: Which desert holds the record for the highest reliably recorded air temperature on Earth at 56.7°C (134°F)?
geography.fact: Death Valley in the Mojave hit 134°F in 1913, though Iran's Lut Desert has hotter ground surfaces.

geography.question: What is the driest non-polar desert on Earth, where some weather stations have never recorded a single drop of rain?
geography.fact: Parts of the Atacama went without rain for over 500 years, yet microbial life persists there.

geography.question: How many degrees Celsius can the temperature swing in a single day in the Sahara Desert — from scorching noon to frigid night?
geography.fact: Sand's low heat capacity causes extreme swings — daytime 50°C can drop near 0°C at night.

geography.question: Which Asian country contains over 17,000 islands, making it the world's largest archipelagic state?
geography.fact: A 2023 survey updated Indonesia's island count to over 17,500, with thousands still uninhabited.

geography.question: The Tsingy de Bemaraha-like razor-sharp karst landscapes of Shilin ('Stone Forest') are found in which Chinese province?
geography.fact: Yunnan's Stone Forest formed 270 million years ago from limestone dissolved by tropical rainfall.

geography.question: Which Central Asian country is home to the 'Door to Hell,' a natural gas crater that has been burning continuously since 1971?
geography.fact: Soviet engineers lit the Darvaza crater expecting it to burn out in weeks — it never stopped.

sports.question: Which NBA player holds the record for most turnovers in a single game with 14, set during a 1978 contest?
sports.fact: John Drew committed 14 turnovers for the Atlanta Hawks — a record that has stood for over 45 years.

sports.question: In the 2025 NBA season, which player became the youngest to record a 50-point triple-double?
sports.fact: Dončić achieved this milestone at just 25, breaking a record many thought belonged to LeBron James.

sports.question: Which NBA arena sits at the highest elevation above sea level?
sports.fact: Ball Arena in Denver sits at roughly 5,280 feet — visiting teams often struggle with the thin air.

sports.question: True or False: Wilt Chamberlain once led the NBA in assists for an entire season despite being a center.
options: He tied for the lead but didn't win it
He led only in assists per game, not total
sports.fact: In 1967-68, Chamberlain averaged 8.6 assists per game — the only center ever to lead the league.

sports.question: Who was the first athlete to break the 10-second barrier in the 100 meters using fully automatic timing?
sports.fact: Jim Hines ran 9.95 seconds at the 1968 Mexico City Olympics — the record stood for 15 years.

sports.question: Which sport saw its first openly professional world championship held before any other major sport, in 1927?
sports.fact: The first World Snooker Championship was openly professional from its inception in 1927 — predating most sports.

sports.question: How many consecutive Tour de France stages did Eddy Merckx win in a row during his record 1969 Tour?
sports.fact: Merckx won 7 consecutive stages in 1969 — a feat of dominance never matched in Tour history.

sports.question: Which boxing legend retired with a record of 49-0 after his final fight against Rocky Marciano… wait — who WAS Rocky Marciano's final opponent?
sports.fact: Marciano knocked out Archie Moore in round 9 on September 21, 1955, then never fought again.

sports.question: Which heavyweight boxing champion held the title for the shortest reign — just 83 days — before losing it?
sports.fact: Buster Douglas shocked the world by beating Tyson but lost his title to Evander Holyfield just 83 days later.

sports.question: Who is the only boxer to have knocked down both Muhammad Ali and Joe Frazier in the same calendar year?
sports.fact: In 1973, Foreman demolished Frazier in January and fought others — but knocked both down across his career that era.

food.question: Which chocolate bar was originally developed in 1932 as a convenient snack for workers that could fit in a lunchbox?
food.fact: Kit Kat was originally called 'Rowntree's Chocolate Crisp' and was marketed as a working man's teatime snack.

food.question: True or False: White chocolate technically isn't chocolate because it contains no cocoa solids.
options: It depends on the country's food laws
food.fact: White chocolate is made from cocoa butter, sugar, and milk but zero cocoa solids — the part that makes chocolate 'chocolate.'

food.question: In 2025, which major chocolate company announced it would begin using lab-grown cocoa butter in select products?
food.fact: Mars partnered with biotech firms to address cocoa supply shortages driven by West African crop failures.

food.question: What is 'conching' in chocolate manufacturing?
options: Roasting cocoa beans at high heat
Prolonged mixing and aerating of liquid chocolate
Coating candy in tempered chocolate shells
food.fact: Conching can last up to 72 hours and was invented by Rodolphe Lindt in 1879 — it's why chocolate is smooth.

food.question: Which traditional British dish consists of sausages baked in a Yorkshire pudding batter?
food.fact: The name's origin is murky, but 'toad' may refer to how sausages peeking from batter resemble toads from holes.

food.question: What unusual ingredient was historically added to British black pudding besides pig's blood?
food.fact: Oatmeal or barley is a key filler in black pudding — Bury in Lancashire claims to make Britain's finest version.

food.question: Which British condiment, first bottled in the 1830s, was originally left forgotten in a cellar where it accidentally fermented into its famous tangy flavor?
food.fact: Lea & Perrins abandoned the batch as inedible — rediscovering it years later to find fermentation perfected the taste.

food.question: Which fast food chain was the first to introduce a drive-through window service in 1948?
food.fact: In-N-Out Burger pioneered the drive-through concept using a two-way speaker system in Baldwin Park, California.

food.question: How many herbs and spices are in KFC's famously secret 'Original Recipe' blend?
food.fact: The recipe is split between two suppliers so neither knows the full blend — it's stored in a Louisville vault.

food.question: Which fast food chain originally started as a barbecue restaurant called 'McDonald's Famous Barbecue' before pivoting to burgers?
food.fact: The McDonald brothers realized 80% of profits came from burgers, so they shut down and relaunched as a burger stand in 1948.

music.question: Which Beatles song was recorded with the tape literally running backward to create its dreamlike effect, a first in pop music?
music.fact: Rain's B-side featured the first deliberately reversed vocal track in pop music history, suggested by a happy accident by Lennon.

music.question: True or False: Pete Best was fired from The Beatles just nine days before the recording of their first single.
music.fact: Pete Best was dismissed on August 16, 1962 — 'Love Me Do' was recorded on September 4, just 19 days later.

music.question: What unusual instrument did George Harrison play on 'Norwegian Wood,' making it the first use of this instrument on a Western pop record?
music.fact: Harrison bought the sitar from a London shop on Tottenham Court Road, initially not knowing how to tune it properly.

music.question: Which Beatles album was the first major pop record to include a complete list of song lyrics on the sleeve?
options: Sgt. Pepper's Lonely Hearts Club Band
music.fact: Sgt. Pepper's printed lyrics were revolutionary — previously, fans had to buy separate lyric sheets or transcribe songs by ear.

music.question: Which 80s pop icon's 2025 reunion tour became the highest-grossing tour by a solo artist over 60, according to Billboard?
music.fact: Cyndi Lauper's 'Girls Just Wanna Have Fun Farewell Tour' broke records, proving the enduring power of 80s nostalgia.

music.question: How many consecutive #1 Billboard Hot 100 singles did Whitney Houston achieve from 1985 to 1988?
music.fact: Whitney's seven consecutive #1 hits surpassed The Beatles' and Bee Gees' records — a feat unmatched until 2018.

music.question: Which 80s synth-pop band recorded their global hit 'Take On Me' and released it THREE times before it became a smash?
music.fact: a-ha's 'Take On Me' flopped twice in 1984 before its iconic pencil-sketch video propelled it to #1 in 1985.

music.question: Singer-songwriter Elliott Smith was posthumously revealed to have secretly played which instrument on a famous Beck album?
music.fact: Smith quietly contributed piano to Beck's heartbreak masterpiece 'Sea Change' without seeking any public credit.

music.question: Which singer-songwriter wrote 'Hallelujah,' which was rejected by his record label and only became iconic years after its initial release?
music.fact: Cohen reportedly wrote around 80 draft verses for 'Hallelujah' — his label initially refused to release the album containing it.

music.question: Who became the youngest singer-songwriter to win the Grammy for Album of the Year twice, achieving the second win before turning 25?
music.fact: Billie Eilish won her second Album of the Year at 22, making her the youngest double winner in Grammy history.

technology.question: Which robot, deployed in 2025 by Amazon, can autonomously sort packages while navigating warehouse workers without safety cages?
technology.fact: Digit, made by Agility Robotics, is one of the first bipedal robots deployed commercially in warehouses.

technology.question: True or False: The world's first robot-performed laparoscopic surgery without any human assistance occurred on soft tissue in a living animal.
options: True — it was performed on pig intestine
False — it was performed on a synthetic organ
True — it was performed on a human patient
False — no such surgery has occurred yet
technology.fact: Johns Hopkins' STAR robot autonomously sutured pig intestines in 2022, outperforming some human surgeons.

technology.question: What counterintuitive problem makes many industrial robots worse at picking up soft objects like lettuce than rigid ones?
options: Compliance control — they can't modulate grip force finely enough
Battery drain from repeated grasping cycles
technology.fact: Soft object manipulation remains robotics' hardest grasping challenge because deformation makes force feedback unpredictable.

technology.question: How many axes of movement does a standard industrial articulated robot arm typically have?
technology.fact: Six axes mimic the degrees of freedom of a human arm, allowing the end effector to reach any orientation.

technology.question: Which programming language, created in 2012, uses an 'ownership' system that eliminates the need for a garbage collector while preventing memory leaks?
technology.fact: Rust's borrow checker enforces memory safety at compile time, making data races impossible without runtime overhead.

technology.question: What was the original name of JavaScript when Brendan Eich first created it at Netscape in 1995?
technology.fact: It was internally called Mocha, then renamed LiveScript, then JavaScript — despite having almost nothing to do with Java.

technology.question: Which language, gaining mainstream traction in 2025, was specifically designed by Google as a successor to C++ for large-scale systems?
technology.fact: Carbon is designed to interoperate seamlessly with existing C++ codebases, easing incremental migration.

technology.question: In cybersecurity, what does a 'confused deputy' attack exploit?
options: A privileged program tricked into misusing its authority
technology.fact: The term originated in 1988 from a compiler service that was tricked into overwriting its own billing file.

technology.question: Which infamous 2024–2025 vulnerability in the XZ Utils library was secretly planted by a trusted open-source contributor over two years?
technology.fact: A pseudonymous contributor patiently gained maintainer trust for years before inserting a sophisticated SSH backdoor.

technology.question: What surprising technique can extract encryption keys from a computer by analyzing its electromagnetic emissions from several meters away?
technology.fact: TEMPEST attacks were classified by the NSA for decades — even CRT monitor images can be reconstructed from radio leaks.

nature.question: Which dog breed has a unique split or 'double' nose, with two distinct nostrils separated by a band of skin?
nature.fact: The Catalburun from Turkey is one of only three breeds worldwide with this split-nose trait.

nature.question: True or False: Goats have rectangular pupils that give them nearly 340-degree panoramic vision.
nature.fact: Their horizontal slit pupils let them see almost all around without moving their heads.

nature.question: In 2025, researchers discovered that domestic cats share a surprising genetic adaptation with which animal for processing fats?
nature.fact: Cats and polar bears independently evolved nearly identical gene mutations for thriving on high-fat diets.

nature.question: How many distinct vocalizations can an average domestic cat produce?
nature.fact: Cats reserve most of their vocal range for communicating with humans, not other cats.

nature.question: Which fungus can control the behavior of ants, forcing them to climb to elevated positions before killing them?
nature.fact: The fungus manipulates ant muscles directly—the brain remains largely unaffected during zombification.

nature.question: What is the largest known living organism on Earth by area, a fungus in Oregon's Blue Mountains?
nature.fact: This honey fungus spans roughly 2,385 acres and is estimated to be over 2,400 years old.

nature.question: Which microorganism can survive in the vacuum of outer space, extreme radiation, and temperatures near absolute zero?
nature.fact: Nicknamed 'Conan the Bacterium,' it can reassemble its shattered DNA within hours of extreme radiation.

nature.question: Which massive prehistoric shark is estimated to have had a bite force exceeding 40,000 pounds?
nature.fact: Megalodon's bite force was roughly ten times stronger than that of a modern great white shark.

nature.question: What surprising feature did the dinosaur Therizinosaurus possess, despite likely being an herbivore?
nature.fact: Its meter-long claws are the longest of any known animal and were probably used for pulling branches.

nature.question: Which prehistoric mammal, often called a 'terror beast,' had claws so large it was once misidentified as a giant ground sloth?
nature.fact: Chalicotherium walked on its knuckles to protect its massive claws, resembling a gorilla-horse hybrid.

film.question: Which silent film star famously insured her legs for $1 million, making them arguably the first celebrity body part insurance policy?
film.fact: Betty Grable's legs were insured with Lloyd's of London, making her pin-up poster iconic in WWII.

film.question: In the 1920 German Expressionist film 'The Cabinet of Dr. Caligari,' what unusual technique was used instead of realistic lighting?
options: Shadows painted directly onto the sets
film.fact: Painted shadows and distorted angles created the nightmarish look without needing complex lighting equipment.

film.question: Who was the first person to receive an Academy Honorary Award (a precursor to the Oscar), given at the very first ceremony in 1929?
film.fact: Chaplin was originally a competitive nominee but was pulled and given a special award instead.

film.question: True or False: Buster Keaton performed the famous falling house facade stunt in 'Steamboat Bill, Jr.' (1928) with only inches of clearance, using no camera tricks.
options: True — a real two-ton wall fell around him
False — it was a lightweight prop wall
False — it used early stop-motion techniques
True — but a stunt double performed it
film.fact: A nail in the ground marked Keaton's exact position; being off by inches would have killed him.

film.question: In the 2025 horror film 'The Monkey,' based on a Stephen King story, who directed and adapted the screenplay?
film.fact: Osgood Perkins followed his hit 'Longlegs' with this darkly comedic King adaptation released in early 2025.

film.question: Which 1977 Italian horror film features a music school hiding a coven of witches, with a soundtrack by the band Goblin?
film.fact: Director Dario Argento used vivid Technicolor-style processing that was nearly obsolete by 1977.

film.question: In 'The Exorcist' (1973), what unusual real-world technique did director William Friedkin use to get genuine startled reactions from actors?
options: Firing a shotgun blank on set without warning
Spraying actors with ice water unexpectedly
Dropping the set temperature to near freezing
film.fact: Friedkin fired blanks and even slapped actors to provoke authentic shock and fear on camera.

film.question: In 'Blade Runner' (1982), how many official different cuts of the film have been released as of 2025?
film.fact: The seven versions include the workprint, US theatrical, international, director's cut, and the 2007 Final Cut.

film.question: Which sci-fi film coined the term 'android' in popular cinema, featuring a female robot named Hel?
film.fact: Fritz Lang's 'Metropolis' robot Maria inspired countless sci-fi androids, including C-3PO's design.

film.question: In the sci-fi film 'Arrival' (2016), the alien written language was based on a concept from which real linguistic hypothesis?
film.fact: The Sapir-Whorf hypothesis suggests language shapes thought — in the film, learning Heptapod literally alters time perception.

politics.question: Which Italian prime minister survived multiple criminal trials including charges of tax fraud, bribery, and connections to the Mafia?
politics.fact: Berlusconi faced over 20 criminal trials during his career yet served as prime minister three separate times.

politics.question: In 2025, which country's ruling party faced a major corruption scandal involving billions in misallocated COVID-era relief funds?
politics.fact: South Africa's SIU investigations revealed over $5 billion in irregular COVID procurement spending across government agencies.

politics.question: Who was the British politician forced to resign in the Profumo Affair of 1963 after lying to Parliament about his relationship with Christine Keeler?
politics.fact: After resigning, Profumo spent the remaining 43 years of his life quietly doing charity work in London's East End.

politics.question: True or False: The Watergate break-in that ended Nixon's presidency was actually the burglars' second break-in at the DNC headquarters.
politics.fact: The first break-in occurred on May 28, 1972, but faulty wiretaps prompted the infamous June 17 return trip.

politics.question: Which country became the first in the world to grant women full national voting rights in 1893?
politics.fact: New Zealand women could vote in 1893 but couldn't stand for parliament until 1919—a 26-year gap.

politics.question: How many countries worldwide are currently classified as 'full democracies' according to the Economist Intelligence Unit's Democracy Index?
politics.fact: Only about 24 countries qualify as full democracies, covering less than 8% of the world's population.

politics.question: Which Pacific island nation uses a unique 'no-party' parliamentary system where all members run as independents?
politics.fact: Palau's constitution doesn't mention political parties, and its legislature functions entirely on individual candidacies.

politics.question: The 1932 Constitutionalist Revolution in Brazil was fought primarily by rebels from which state against the federal government?
politics.fact: São Paulo's revolt lasted only three months but mobilized over 100,000 fighters using locally manufactured armored trains.

politics.question: Which uprising saw Paris workers establish a revolutionary socialist government that lasted 72 days in 1871?
politics.fact: The Paris Commune introduced free secular education and equal pay for teachers—radical ideas that took decades elsewhere.

politics.question: The 2019 Sudanese revolution overthrew Omar al-Bashir after how many years in power?
politics.fact: Al-Bashir was the first sitting head of state ever indicted by the International Criminal Court, in 2009.
